content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/digital-services-act", "title": "The Digital Services Act | Shaping Europe's digital future", "summary": "The Digital Services Act helps to make the online environment safe and trustworthy. The Digital Services Act (DSA) introduces rules for online services used by European citizens in their everyday life. These services include marketplaces, social media networks, app stores, and online travel and accommodation platforms.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Platforms must respond to your reports and offer options to appeal their decisions. - Enhanced protection for minors - Platforms must take measures to safeguard minors on their services, such as reducing the risks of exposure to age-inappropriate con", "For the same reason, the DSA requires marketplaces to make reasonable efforts to perform random checks on products sold on their service or adopt new technologies for product traceability."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Platforms must respond to your reports and offer options to appeal their decisions. - Enhanced protection for minors - P"}, {"url": "https://cjil.uchicago.edu/print-archive/digital-services-act-and-brussels-effect-platform-content-moderation", "title": "The Digital Services Act and the Brussels Effect on Platform Content ...", "summary": "The Digital Services Act and the Brussels Effect on Platform Content Moderation The EU\u2019s latest regulation of social media platforms\u2014the Digital Services Act (DSA)\u2014will create tension and conflict with the U.S. speech regime applicable to social media platforms. The DSA, like prior EU regulations of social media platforms, will further instantiate the Brussels Effect, whereby EU regulators wield powerful influence on how social media platforms moderate content on the global scale. This is because the DSA\u2019s regulatory regime (with its huge penalties for noncompliance) will incentivize the platforms to skew their global content moderation policies toward the EU\u2019s instead of the U.S.\u2019s balance of speech harms versus benefits.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["This is because the DSA levies huge financial penalties for violating its provisions, including maximum fines of six percent of a platform\u2019s annual worldwide turnover.2 Commission Regulation 2022/2065, Shaping Europe\u2019s Digital Future: The Digital Ser", "The DSA, which entered into force on November 16, 2022, and became operational on very large social media platforms in early 2023, maintains the conditional liability regime imposed over two decades ago under the EU E-Commerce Directive.3 Id.; Counci", "As defined in the DSA, \u201c\u2018illegal content\u2019 means any information that . . . is not in compliance with Union law or the law of any Member State . . . irrespective of the precise subject matter or nature of that law. . . .\u201d Id. art. 3(h).", "See id. art. 16(1). \u201cProviders of hosting services shall put mechanisms in place to allow any individual or entity to notify them of the presence on their service of specific items of information that the individual or entity considers to be illegal ", "Thus, upon receiving notice of allegedly illegal content hosted on their platform, providers must expeditiously remove such content\u2014or risk liability.10 Id."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: This is because the DSA levies huge financial penalties for violating its provisions, including maximum fines of six per"}, {"url": "https://www.europeansources.info/record/proposal-for-a-regulation-on-a-single-market-for-digital-services-digital-services-act-and-amending-directive-2000-31-ec/", "title": "Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 on a Single Market For Digital Services ...", "summary": "| Summary: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 - formally adopted by the co-legislators on 19 October 2022 - setting up a framework for regulating digital services in the European Union (EU). This is also known as the Digital Services Act (DSA). It introduces a number of amendments to Directive 2000/31/EC, also known as e-Commerce Directive.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["| Summary: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 - formally adopted by the co-legislators on 19 October 2022 - setting up a framework for regulating digital services in the European Union (EU).", "It introduces a number of amendments to Directive 2000/31/EC, also known as e-Commerce Directive.", "Further information: This Regulation applies to intermediary services offered to recipients of the service that have their place of establishment or are located in the EU, irrespective of where the providers of those intermediary services have their ", "Since the adoption of Directive 2000/31/EC, new and innovative digital services emerged, shaping and transforming how citizens communicate, connect, consume and do business.", "The draft law was eventually tabled by the European Commission on 15 December 2020 as part of the so-called a legislative package which also included the proposed Digital Markets Act (DMA)."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: | Summary: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 - formally adopted by the co-legislators on 19 October 2022 - setting up a framewor"}, {"url": "https://wilmap.stanford.edu/node/31156", "title": "Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 of the European Parliament and of the ...", "summary": "The DSA represents and overhaul of EU law governing intermediaries\u2019 handling of user content. It builds on the pre-existing eCommerce Directive from 2000, and preserves key ideas and legal structures from that law. The DSA applies to numerous Internet intermediary services.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["It builds on the pre-existing eCommerce Directive from 2000, and preserves key ideas and legal structures from that law.", "It allows enforcers to assess extremely steep fines, in principle reaching up to 6% of annual revenue.", "However, despite significant civil society opposition, the final draft apparently suggests a period of 24 hours for illegal hate speech. - Notifying users when their content has been taken down or otherwise acted against, providing an internal appeal"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: It builds on the pre-existing eCommerce Directive from 2000, and preserves key ideas and legal structures from that law."}, {"url": "https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/17577632.2024.2408912", "title": "Toxic recommender algorithms: immunities, liabilities and the regulated self-regulation of the Digital Services Act and the Online Safety Act", "summary": "This debate is beset by issues, including defining 'online harms', respecting freedom of expression, and others. While much of this debate has focused on content hosted by online platforms, comparatively little attention has been paid to the central role of algorithmic personalisation \u2013 or \u2018recommending\u2019 \u2013 by platforms in content dissemination in online environments and the problems to which this... Illegal pricing algorithms Communications of the ACM , 2018 Examining the potential legal consequences of uses of pricing algorithms.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["While much of this debate has focused on content hosted by online platforms, comparatively little attention has been paid to the central role of algorithmic personalisation \u2013 or \u2018recommending\u2019 \u2013 by platforms in content dissemination in online environ", "Illegal pricing algorithms Communications of the ACM , 2018 Examining the potential legal consequences of uses of pricing algorithms.", "Recommendation in social networks Proceedings of the 7th ACM conference on Recommender systems , 2013 No abstract available.", "DRM and privacy Communications of the ACM , 2003 How should the law respond to DRM restrictions that invade user privacy?", "Regulating Digital Harm Across Borders: Exploring a Content Platform Commission International Conference on Social Media and Society , 2020 Regulatory systems crafted in and for countries with well-functioning, well-intentioned democratic governments"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: While much of this debate has focused on content hosted by online platforms, comparatively little attention has been pai"}]}
